title: Week 1 Data Lab: Loading and Visualizing Data Structures
text:
Lab Overview

In this practical lab, we will load different data tables and visualize their structures as discussed in the theoretical session. We will explore how Cross-Sectional, Time Series, and Panel data look in R.

title: Week 1 Data Lab: Loading and Visualizing Data Structures
text:
Setting up the environment
We start by loading the tidyverse package, which provides a cohesive set of tools for data manipulation and visualization.

# Load necessary libraries
library(tidyverse)

title: Week 1 Data Lab: Loading and Visualizing Data Structures
Week 1 Data Lab: Loading and Visualizing Data Structures
text:
2. Time Series Data
Time series data consist of observations of one or more variables over time. Here we use the economics dataset from ggplot2.

# Load time series data
data(economics)

# Visualize structure
glimpse(economics)

Rows: 574
Columns: 6
$ date     <date> 1967-07-01, 1967-08-01, 1967-09-01, 1967-10-01, 1967-11-01, …
$ pce      <dbl> 506.7, 509.8, 515.6, 512.2, 517.4, 525.1, 530.9, 533.6, 544.3…
$ pop      <dbl> 198712, 198911, 199113, 199311, 199498, 199657, 199808, 19992…
$ psavert  <dbl> 12.6, 12.6, 11.9, 12.9, 12.8, 11.8, 11.7, 12.3, 11.7, 12.3, 1…
$ uempmed  <dbl> 4.5, 4.7, 4.6, 4.9, 4.7, 4.8, 5.1, 4.5, 4.1, 4.6, 4.4, 4.4, 4…
$ unemploy <dbl> 2944, 2945, 2958, 3143, 3066, 3018, 2878, 3001, 2877, 2709, 2…

# Plotting time series structure quickly
ggplot(economics, aes(x = date, y = psavert)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(title = "Personal Savings Rate Over Time", x = "Date", y = "Savings Rate")

title: Week 1 Data Lab: Loading and Visualizing Data Structures
text:
3. Panel or Longitudinal Data
Panel data follows the same elements over multiple time periods. We can simulate a simple structure to understand how it organizes individuals across time.

# Creating a simple mock panel dataset
set.seed(123)
panel_data <- tibble(
  individual_id = rep(1:5, each = 3),
  year = rep(2021:2023, times = 5),
  income = round(runif(15, 30000, 80000), 0)
)

# Visualize structure
glimpse(panel_data)

Rows: 15
Columns: 3
$ individual_id <int> 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5
$ year          <int> 2021, 2022, 2023, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2021, 2022, 2023, 20…
$ income        <dbl> 44379, 69415, 50449, 74151, 77023, 32278, 56405, 74621, …

# Show first few rows
head(panel_data)

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  individual_id  year income
          <int> <int>  <dbl>
1             1  2021  44379
2             1  2022  69415
3             1  2023  50449
4             2  2021  74151
5             2  2022  77023
6             2  2023  32278

title: Week 1: Introduction and Data Structures
text:
Session Overview

This session introduces the foundational concepts of quantitative methods and the structure of data. We explore what variables are, their different types, causality, and how data is structured for analysis.

title: Week 1: Introduction and Data Structures
text:
1. What is a Variable?
In quantitative methods, a variable is any characteristic, number, or quantity that can be measured or counted. A variable may also be called a data item. Examples include age, sex, business income and expenses, country of birth, capital expenditure, class grades, and eye color.

title: Week 1: Introduction and Data Structures
text:
2. Types of Variables
Variables are broadly classified into two categories:

Categorical (Qualitative) Variables: Describe a quality or characteristic.

Nominal: Categories with no inherent order (e.g., eye color, gender).
Ordinal: Categories with a logical order or rank (e.g., education level, satisfaction rating).

Numeric (Quantitative) Variables: Represent a measurable quantity.

Discrete: Can only take specific, separate values (e.g., number of students in a class).
Continuous: Can take any value within a range (e.g., height, weight, time).

title: Week 1: Introduction and Data Structures
text:
3. Causality vs. Correlation
A fundamental concept in quantitative methods is distinguishing between correlation (two variables changing together) and causality (one variable causing the change in another). Understanding this distinction is crucial to avoid drawing incorrect conclusions from data patterns.

title: Week 1: Introduction and Data Structures
text:
4. AI Usage in Quantitative Analysis
Artificial Intelligence and machine learning tools are increasingly used to assist in data analysis. AI can help in: * Automating data cleaning and preprocessing. * Identifying complex patterns in large datasets. * Generating preliminary code for statistical models. * Note: Always verify AI-generated insights against statistical principles.

title: Week 1: Introduction and Data Structures
text:
5. Data Structures
Before analyzing data, we must understand how it is structured. The three main structures we study are:

Cross-Sectional Data: Observations of a sample at one specific point in time (e.g., survey results from a single year).
Time Series Data: Observations of one or more variables over time (e.g., daily stock prices over a month).
Panel (Longitudinal) Data: Observations of the same subjects over multiple time periods (e.g., tracking the income of the same group of individuals over five years).



Cross-References


Proceed to the Week 1 Data Lab to learn how to load and visualize these data structures in R.
